items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: Which document sets out measurable criteria a product must meet?
items.why: It's Specification. “Brief” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Repeated cycles of refinement. “Single linear process” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many stages are typical in a basic design cycle (research, design, develop, prototype, test, evaluate)?
items.why: The real figure is 6 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
